title: The Olive Branch

jokes.text: Moira presents Alexis's college certificate — but the name is struck through due to low toner, calling back to the orthodontist certificate
jokes.setup: Moira proudly presents Alexis's hard-earned college certificate 'hot off the presses'
jokes.punchline: The name is crossed out due to low toner — a comedic deflation of the celebratory moment
jokes.explanation: A solid visual gag that deflates the ceremonial moment immediately. The toner excuse is mundane and bathetic, which is the point. Works as character comedy because Moira's obliviousness to how underwhelming this is defines her.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, this is your official college certificate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that a line through my name?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the printer hasn't been serviced in quite some time.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So it literally says I didn't go to college.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Technically, it says you did go to college, there's just a line through it.

jokes.text: This certificate reminds me of the one you were awarded for getting your braces off.
jokes.setup: Moira is meant to be celebrating Alexis's college degree
jokes.punchline: She equates it to a certificate for getting braces removed — implying she views both as equally trivial accomplishments
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira character comedy. The specificity of the 'braces off certificate' is brilliantly absurd — it implies Moira routinely awarded certificates for minor milestones, and now can't distinguish between those and a college degree. Only Moira could deliver this with full sincerity. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A college degree is a major achievement, but so is completing orthodontic treatment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, those are not the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why not? You wear the brackets for years, you follow all the rules, you finally get them off, and you have something to show for it.

jokes.text: Yes, except my orthodontist had a slightly better printer.
jokes.setup: Moira has just compared Alexis's college certificate to the orthodontist one
jokes.punchline: Alexis accepts the comparison and turns it into a dig at the current certificate's quality
jokes.explanation: Crisp comeback that plays along with Moira's absurd framing rather than correcting it. The economy is near-perfect — a lot of character information conveyed in one dry line. Alexis doesn't protest the comparison, she just shades the execution. Smart writing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, my orthodontist certificate has way better printing than my college diploma.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's because your orthodontist takes their credentials seriously.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, did David spend four months earning a degree?
jokes.setup: Moira says 'we're equally proud of you both'
jokes.punchline: Alexis pointedly notes David did NOT earn a degree — suggesting Moira is extending equal praise to unequal accomplishments
jokes.explanation: Competent sibling rivalry joke. Alexis calling out Moira's equivocation is in character and gets a solid laugh, but it's a fairly standard 'mom treats us equally when I did more work' beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just got offered my own PR firm, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... that's great, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know it is. And you're still just... doing whatever it is you do at the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I own the store, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Pubic relations.
jokes.setup: Moira reads Alexis's certificate aloud: 'majoring in Marketing and...'
jokes.punchline: The certificate reads 'Pubic Relations' instead of 'Public Relations'
jokes.explanation: A classic misprint gag. The typo itself is an old joke, but the structure is impeccable — the pause before reveal, Moira's flat delivery, Alexis's horror, and then David's 'Is it though, do you think?' elevates it considerably. Not original but well-executed and the scene reactions make it land big.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It says here you have a degree in Pubic Relations.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No, it's Public Relations. There's a typo on the degree.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's certainly a credential that precedes you.

jokes.text: Is it though, do you think?
jokes.setup: Moira says the 'Pubic Relations' is obviously a misprint
jokes.punchline: David, with characteristic passive-aggression toward Alexis, muses aloud whether it might actually be accurate
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the cold open. Four words that accomplish everything: it's in perfect David character (petty, dry, gleeful in sibling rivalry), it builds on the setup, it's maximally economical, and it's instantly quotable. The pause implied before delivery makes it land even harder. Earns its high score — comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's obviously a misprint.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is it though? What if it's intentional?

jokes.text: David, do not diminish your sister's accomplishments. She didn't do anything to your [certificate].
jokes.setup: Moira scolds David for implying Alexis might legitimately study 'pubic relations'
jokes.punchline: She defends Alexis while inadvertently validating David's implication — 'she didn't DO anything' to your certificate suggesting Alexis's choices are beyond reproach in ways that invite speculation
jokes.explanation: Moira's defense is slightly garbled (the transcript is cut off), which itself may be comedic. Her authoritative defense of Alexis while missing the joke is on-brand but the execution here is muddled.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you can't wear that.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because you have pockets. And pockets are a cry for help.

jokes.text: I wanted to frame this! [holds up the 'Pubic Relations' certificate] It looks good!
jokes.setup: Alexis is mortified by the misprint on her certificate
jokes.punchline: Someone (Johnny) admires it earnestly — the certificate with the embarrassing misprint is held up and declared 'it looks good'
jokes.explanation: Functional button on the cold open joke. Johnny's oblivious positivity is classic but not especially crafted here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was going to confront him sooner, but I wasn't ready. I wasn't ready. Okay, I wasn't ready.

jokes.text: So you're wearing a leather sweater, in the dead of summer.
jokes.setup: David arrives at Stevie's to announce he's ready to reconcile with Patrick
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately clocks his outfit choice — leather in summer — as a tell that he's trying too hard
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven observational humor. Stevie's deadpan reading of David through his fashion choices is a well-established comedic dynamic. The specificity of 'leather sweater in the dead of summer' adds texture.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You just like being treated like royalty.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I do not enjoy being treated like royalty.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you literally just said—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I enjoy being treated like *me*. There's a difference.

jokes.text: Okay, I just wanted to make sure that I was ready, ready.
jokes.setup: Stevie points out David said two days ago he was going to go tell Patrick he was ready
jokes.punchline: David distinguishes between being 'ready' and being 'ready, ready' — a sophistic distinction that reveals his stalling
jokes.explanation: Charming self-aware line. The doubling of 'ready' is a relatable yet David-specific rationalization. Earns its score through character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: People think you guys should work things out because you're obviously still in love with each other.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not... I don't think that's...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, first you were just texting him a lot, which was cute, then you were showing up at his place, which was a little needy, and now you're just... following him around.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not following him.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally followed him to the farmer's market today.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I needed produce.

jokes.text: That's a nice bracelet. Did you get that from Patrick?
jokes.setup: David is explaining his delay in reconciling with Patrick for 'both of their sakes'
jokes.punchline: Stevie cuts right through with a pointed observation about David's new bracelet — implying he's been enjoying the apology gifts
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy. Stevie's casual pivot to the bracelet completely dismantles David's elaborate justification without arguing with it. Classic straight-man puncturing of self-deception. The non sequitur structure makes it land harder.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not saying I don't not want to reconcile with you.

jokes.text: So you waiting a full week to forgive him has nothing to do with the fact that he showered you with gifts and attention every day. That is purely coincidental!
jokes.setup: David has an elaborate explanation for why he waited a week to reconcile
jokes.punchline: Stevie flatly states the obvious truth — David was milking the gifts — and David's flustered 'That is purely coincidental!' confirms it
jokes.explanation: Solid comedic exchange. Stevie's flat accusation and David's overdone denial ('That is purely coincidental!') are a classic comedy pair. The character truth — David absolutely would milk apology gifts — makes it earned.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not motivated by gifts.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, can you please make sure to protect any incoming packages?

jokes.text: Maybe needy's the wrong word. Clingy?
jokes.setup: Stevie has called David 'a smidge needy' over the past week
jokes.punchline: She 'corrects' herself to 'clingy' — which is not better and possibly worse — as if she's being helpful
jokes.explanation: Textbook Stevie deadpan. The fake self-correction to a synonymous (or worse) descriptor is a comedy classic, and her delivery of 'Clingy?' as an offering is perfectly calibrated. Economy is high.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Words? David, she's printed words on paper. How pedestrian.

jokes.text: You sure you don't wanna wait another day, though? I mean you never know what could show up on your doorstep.
jokes.setup: David says he's going to go reconcile with Patrick
jokes.punchline: Stevie hints that waiting could mean more gifts arriving — a wink at David's actual motivation for delaying
jokes.explanation: Good callback that extends the gift-milking joke. Stevie's barely-concealed trolling of David is in great character. Not exceptional but competently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you're throwing your High-Street beret in the ring, then I suppose I'll have to dust off my competition gloves. Though I must say, the competition around here is rather... pedestrian.

jokes.text: I don't like what you're insinuating. That said, if a package does arrive, please keep it safe.
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests David should wait another day for more gifts
jokes.punchline: David protests the insinuation... then immediately asks her to safeguard any arriving packages — completely undermining his denial
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The two-beat structure — protest, then immediate contradiction — is perfectly constructed. 'Please keep it safe' is the funniest possible pivot. It's deeply in David's character, it requires zero explanation, and it's highly quotable. Approaches the 8.0 anchor benchmark.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, darling, this isn't a business proposal, it's a cry for help wrapped in spanandry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's not even a word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It is now. And it describes exactly what you're doing here.

jokes.text: And what, pray tell, might this collection of words be?
jokes.setup: Alexis hands Moira her first business proposal for the Arts and Culture Grant
jokes.punchline: Moira receives it with theatrical suspicion, calling it 'a collection of words' — implying it could barely qualify as a document
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira vocabulary comedy. 'Pray tell' plus 'collection of words' is a beautifully dismissive phrasing that only Moira would use. High character integration — this line couldn't come from anyone else.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, there's an old Irish saying: 'May your socks never get damp and your eyebrows stay exactly where you put them.'

jokes.text: Now, don't get me wrong, there is no High-Street beret that I would rather have thrown in my ring, but, I have a funny feeling Council already has its favourite.
jokes.setup: Moira congratulates Alexis on her business proposal but must also manage expectations
jokes.punchline: She compliments Alexis extravagantly ('no beret I'd rather have in my ring') while simultaneously warning it's rigged — both condescending and oddly sincere
jokes.explanation: Moira's mangled idioms ('beret in my ring') are a reliable comedic device and this is a solid execution. The compliment-disguised-as-warning is very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have instituted an Employee of the Month program at the store. It's a wonderful way to boost morale and recognize excellence. And I'm proud to say that I won it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I celebrated alone in my office with a store-brand sparkling cider and a single cupcake I bought from the grocery section.

jokes.text: Oh, very well, Alexis, let's hear your elevator pitch. But bear in mind, I have to get off on the next floor.
jokes.setup: Moira agrees to hear Alexis's business pitch
jokes.punchline: She takes the 'elevator pitch' metaphor literally — or plays it that way — announcing she has to 'get off on the next floor,' which both lands the pun and implies she'll give Alexis almost no time
jokes.explanation: Smart wordplay delivered in full Moira theatricality. Extending the elevator metaphor is both a pun and a joke about Moira's fleeting attention span. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was just joking about the award. I didn't actually give it to myself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, that's somehow worse.

jokes.text: Okay, um, it's a singles night at the café. And the goal is, um, to get people from nearby communities together, for like romance and nibblies.
jokes.setup: Alexis is delivering her professional business pitch
jokes.punchline: 'Nibblies' as a key component of a business proposal to an arts council — the gap between the professional framing and the casual content is the joke
jokes.explanation: Alexis's vocabulary — 'nibblies' — doing the heavy lifting. The gap between her professional ambition and her actual language is funny and characterful. Not a huge joke but earns its score.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You got me a car?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's not just any car. It's a Range Rover.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Johnny, this is insane. I can't accept this.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Nonsense. You deserve it. Besides, I got one too.

jokes.text: Darling, I realize the pickings are slim in this rural spanandry, but you can't ask Council to misuse public funds just so that you can find yourself a new boy toy.
jokes.setup: Alexis pitches a singles night as a legitimate business idea
jokes.punchline: Moira coins 'spanandry' (presumably 'scarcity of men' / spandex + andry?) and accuses Alexis of engineering the event to find a boyfriend — while completely missing the business case
jokes.explanation: Moira inventing words is one of the show's signature comedic pleasures, and 'spanandry' (likely a portmanteau of 'sparse' and 'andry'/men) is a standout example. Combined with her flat accusation that this is about finding Alexis a 'boy toy,' this is a great Moira moment — dismissive, theatrical, and uniquely her.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well? Aren't you going to thank me?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thank you, Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're welcome. I knew you'd love it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

jokes.text: As the Irish like to say, it's a cinch to mash the Murphy where there's love.
jokes.setup: Moira is wrapping up her dismissal of Alexis's pitch
jokes.punchline: She invents a fake Irish proverb — clearly nonsense, delivered with complete conviction — possibly garbling 'easy to mash potatoes' (Murphy = potato)
jokes.explanation: A top-tier Moira moment. The invented Irish proverb is complete nonsense delivered with total authority — 'mash the Murphy' (potato slang) wrapped in fake wisdom. It's the perfect encapsulation of Moira's oblivious pomposity. High originality, perfect character integration, deeply quotable. Earns its near-8 craft score.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You didn't say that.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I didn't have to. Sometimes what you don't say says more than what you do.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's insane. Words are literally designed to communicate.

jokes.text: Ugh! [Stevie presents David with a gift box]
jokes.setup: David has just declared he's going to reconcile with Patrick
jokes.punchline: Another gift has arrived from Patrick — the reaction beat of David's 'Ugh!' (desire vs. principle colliding) and Stevie's knowing presentation of it
jokes.explanation: Good physical/reaction beat that pays off the gift-milking running gag. The 'Ugh!' captures David's internal conflict perfectly. Works better on screen than on the page.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The council meeting was absolutely glud. I've never seen such unasinous support for the new initiative, and frankly, the bombilating energy in that room was simply transcendent.

jokes.text: Ugh! (Alexis's exasperated reaction to Moira's fake proverb)
jokes.setup: Moira has just delivered an invented Irish proverb as a closing statement
jokes.punchline: Alexis's wordless 'Ugh!' is the perfect button — her total defeat in the face of Moira's theatrical nonsense
jokes.explanation: A functional reaction beat that closes the Moira/Alexis pitch scene. Not a standalone joke — it's entirely dependent on joke #19 — but the timing of Alexis's inarticulate defeat amplifies the preceding laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I hate to break it to ya, but you can't just put a bunch of fancy sculptures in the middle of town. This isn't some big city. We got our mitts full just keeping the roads from turning into rivers every time it rains.

jokes.text: Congratulations, Employee of the Month? Since when has this been a thing?
jokes.setup: Johnny has given Stevie a gift to recognize her hard work at the motel
jokes.punchline: It's an 'Employee of the Month' certificate — but this is a tiny family motel with a handful of staff, making the formal award absurdly grandiose
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist humor playing on the gap between the modest motel context and the corporate formality of 'Employee of the Month.' Stevie's bewilderment is well-earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think we should abandon this whole singles idea. It's pedestrian. Instead, we should focus on a sophisticated demographic. You know, like the Scotch. They're infamously disorganized, which means they'll be grateful for any structure we provide.

jokes.text: Well, this is the second month. I won it last month, took myself out for lunch.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks when 'Employee of the Month' became a thing
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals he started it last month and awarded it to himself — completely undermining the gesture's meaning
jokes.explanation: Excellent Johnny Rose comedy. The reveal that he invented the award and gave it to himself first is perfectly timed and deeply in character — his sincere, oblivious corporate instincts applied to a tiny motel. 'Took myself out for lunch' is the perfect button. High economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You need to get these people out there! They're loose! They need to be loose! Let them be loose!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I understand the intention, but perhaps we could use a different word?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Loose! Loose! These people need to get LOOSE!

jokes.text: I'm kidding. It's a little incentive for a job well done.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just said he won Employee of the Month last month and took himself to lunch
jokes.punchline: He claims he was kidding — but the joke is that he probably wasn't, and 'a little incentive for a job well done' is delivered with utter sincerity that suggests it really is how he thinks
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm kidding' walk-back is itself funny because it doesn't fully land — we suspect he wasn't kidding. Works as a character beat but not a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so these are my business receipts. This one is for a domain name, very legitimate. This one is for a photoshoot with a professional photographer. And then... it just devolves into screenshots of inspirational quotes I found on Pinterest and paid someone to design into graphics.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're writing off motivational posters as a business expense?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They're for the brand, David. My brand.

jokes.text: Uh... this seems like... some makeup? It's like a briefcase full of makeup.
jokes.setup: Johnny presents Stevie with what he considers a great gift
jokes.punchline: The gift is a large, fully-stocked briefcase of cosmetics — Stevie's flat, uncomprehending reaction to the absurdly inappropriate gift is the joke
jokes.explanation: The visual reveal of the makeup briefcase is strong. Stevie's escalating description ('some makeup... a briefcase full of makeup') mirrors how wrong the gift is. Her deadpan processing is very on-brand. The visual element would make this funnier on screen.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is there something off about my appearance?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, your hair extensions are showing, your spray tan is uneven, and those false lashes are doing that thing where they're separating from your eyelid.

jokes.text: Because you deserve it. [pause] I see. Well, I'm gonna save this for a special occasion. No, no, no, it's for everyday use. It's what the woman in the store told me.
jokes.setup: Stevie is processing the inappropriate makeup gift
jokes.punchline: Johnny insisting it's for 'everyday use' and citing 'the woman in the store' reveals he took beauty advice from a stranger for a woman he clearly doesn't understand — escalating the wrongness
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy escalation. The reveal that Johnny consulted a stranger rather than thinking about Stevie personally makes the bad gift even worse. His sincerity amplifies the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My dad once gave David a timeshare. For a prison.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: A timeshare for a prison?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And when we all told him how insane that was, he didn't apologize. He just gave David a book about prisons the next Christmas.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So his solution to a bad gift was... more prison content?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's called doubling down, Stevie. It's a Rose family tradition.

jokes.text: Stevie? Aren't you forgetting something? Uh, I just... just don't know how to thank you. Oh, that smile on your face is all I need.
jokes.setup: Stevie says 'Thanks' and tries to walk away from the awkward gift moment
jokes.punchline: Johnny stops her — and Stevie painfully forces a smile and scripted gratitude while clearly mortified, with Johnny obliviously satisfied
jokes.explanation: A satisfying cringe beat. Johnny wanting the grateful reaction and getting a forced one while being completely satisfied is both funny and a little touching. The 'that smile on your face is all I need' is the kind of oblivious sincerity that defines Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't understand why you're all looking at me like that. I'm perfectly capable of playing basketball.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you literally tripped over the ball before the game even started.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was a strategic move to throw off the other team.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, the only thing you threw off was your dignity when you fell into the bleachers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going back out there, and I'm going to show everyone what I'm made of.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but maybe take off the cashmere first?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. This is who I—

jokes.text: Okay, s... so you would like to focus on the store, then? [long beat] I think that's probably a good idea. But hey, it's good to have you back.
jokes.setup: David has arrived to reconcile but Patrick has preemptively apologized and suggested focusing on business, and David went along with it
jokes.punchline: The excruciating pause as both realize what just happened — neither got what they wanted — is the joke, punctuated by Patrick's weak 'it's good to have you back'
jokes.explanation: Strong awkward silence comedy. The two characters have spectacularly miscommunicated their way to a worse outcome, and the pause lets the audience sit in the disaster. Patrick's deflating 'it's good to have you back' is a perfect understatement button.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So David's Bar Mitzvah wish wasn't some spiritual thing or whatever?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I wanted a nose job.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's so sad. You were like thirteen and already thinking about your appearance?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Says the girl who got highlights in middle school.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's different. That's fashion. That's not... mutilating your face.

jokes.text: Well, I'm gonna go ahead and file Ray's Ham and Clam Bake in the t'won't happen pile.
jokes.setup: Council is reviewing grant proposals
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses 'Ray's Ham and Clam Bake' with invented vocabulary — 't'won't happen pile' — delivered with theatrical authority
jokes.explanation: 'T'won't happen pile' is a great Moira coinage — archaic contraction, absolute authority. The specificity of 'Ray's Ham and Clam Bake' as the rejected idea adds texture. Efficient and very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David gave me a diamond bracelet for my birthday.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That sounds nice.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It was white gold. I only wear yellow gold.

jokes.text: Hmm. After a glud of unasinous ideas put forth today, the room is suddenly bombilating with anticipation.
jokes.setup: Moira is about to present her own grant proposal
jokes.punchline: She invents multiple words ('glud,' 'unasinous,' 'bombilating') in a single sentence while self-servingly describing the build-up to her own idea
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Moira Rose vocabulary comedy. 'Glud,' 'unasinous,' and 'bombilating' in rapid succession, all deployed to flatter her own incoming idea, is peak character work. 'Bombilating' is a real word (buzzing/humming) but used here so grandiosely it feels invented. This is among the episode's best jokes — pure Moira in distilled form. Earns its 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was helping an elderly woman cross the street.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which, I realize, sounds like a lie because kindness and generosity are foreign experiences for me.

jokes.text: I'd like us all to close our eyes, and picture 3 to 5... hundred acres of carefully-manicured lawn.
jokes.setup: Moira asks Council to visualize her arts project
jokes.punchline: The pause between '3 to 5' and '...hundred' reveals the scale has inflated by a factor of 100 — a small-town council being asked to imagine hundreds of acres is absurdly out of scale
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural comedy. The deliberate pause mid-sentence before 'hundred' is a beautifully constructed reveal. The audience lands the joke at the same moment the council does. Moira's obliviousness to the absurd scale of her ask is perfect character comedy. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was so upset, I could barely finish my Cristal. I could barely finish my Hermès scarf. I could barely finish my weekend home in the Hamptons.

jokes.text: Uh... yeah, I gotta say, you're not gonna find a bigger Noguchi-head than me, but I think I speak for everyone here when I say, good luck on getting your mitts on a Von Schlegell!
jokes.setup: Moira has presented her grand arts park vision featuring works by Noguchi, Von Schlegell, and Abakanowicz
jokes.punchline: Ronnie, the most pragmatic council member, responds to world-class sculpture ambitions with 'you're not gonna find a bigger Noguchi-head than me' — absurdly claiming expertise — then shoots it down on logistical grounds
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character moments. 'You're not gonna find a bigger Noguchi-head than me' is brilliantly absurd — Ronnie claiming enthusiasm for avant-garde sculpture before shooting it down is funny on multiple levels. The specificity of 'Von Schlegell' as the logistical obstacle is perfect. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying I need to apologize twice?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No, David. One apology. One gift is sufficient.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But I already bought the second gift...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Then return it.

jokes.text: I recently heard of a small town in Scotland, no bigger than a thimble, that is making millions from a week-long singles fest. Now, if an idea as simple as that can work for a people as infamously disorganized as the Scotch...
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to show Rosewood could scale like other successful small-town events
jokes.punchline: She misremembers the country (Alexis said Ireland, not Scotland) and refers to Scottish people as 'the Scotch' — an outdated, slightly offensive demonym delivered with breezy authority
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one: Moira getting the country wrong (Scotland vs. Ireland) and calling them 'the Scotch.' The casual authority with which she says 'the Scotch' and 'infamously disorganized' is wonderful Moira obliviousness. Good callback value.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I may have been slightly misleading about the chocolates. I didn't just finish them. I devoured them. Completely. I ate the entire box.

jokes.text: Now there's an idea. Thank you! Ronnie's on board with Rosewood. No, I'm not! I like the singles fest.
jokes.setup: Ronnie responds positively to Moira's singles week example
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately claims Ronnie is endorsing Rosewood; Ronnie shoots her down instantly — she liked the singles idea, not Moira's art park
jokes.explanation: Sharp comedic beat. Moira's instant spin on Ronnie's support, followed by Ronnie's equally instant deflation, is tight and funny. The speed of the exchange is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, wait. Before you go, I need you to pick up some things from the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I'd prefer if you took the car that doesn't have the squeaky suspension.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, and could you grab my dry cleaning? The place closes at six.

jokes.text: Single people is what this town needs! Loose wallets, loose inhibitions, everything's just really loose.
jokes.setup: Council is debating the singles event vs. Rosewood
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's pitch for singles week is entirely mercenary and slightly suggestive: 'loose wallets, loose inhibitions, everything's just really loose'
jokes.explanation: The escalation of 'loose' — wallets, inhibitions, everything — building into an unintentional (or intentional) innuendo is clever. Ronnie's pragmatic worldliness is perfectly captured. 'Everything's just really loose' is quotable and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I look like I'm ready to work the pole at a strip club.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you'd be very successful.

jokes.text: And a few pages of temporary letterhead. And um, a list of Buzzfeed's most motivational quotes for girl bosses under 30.
jokes.setup: Alexis is using the Council printer for business items
jokes.punchline: Her 'business' printing includes a Buzzfeed girl boss quote list — the gap between her CEO aspirations and her actual materials is the joke
jokes.explanation: Solid character joke. 'Buzzfeed's most motivational quotes for girl bosses under 30' is specific and funny as a detail of Alexis's brand of entrepreneurship. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Stevie, gift-giving is a delicate art. It requires thought, consideration, and an understanding of the recipient's needs and desires.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Which is why I'm confused about this basketball court.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That was one time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It was a very generous gift, but perhaps not as well thought out as you might have hoped.

jokes.text: I'm gonna do something uncharacteristic, and ask your advice. Is there something off about the way that I look?
jokes.setup: Stevie approaches Alexis looking for advice about the makeup gift
jokes.punchline: She prefaces it with 'I'm gonna do something uncharacteristic' — framing asking for advice as deeply out of character, which is both self-aware and funny about Stevie's persona
jokes.explanation: Nice self-aware character beat. Stevie flagging that seeking advice is uncharacteristic for her is a good moment of self-knowledge played for laughs.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so it's like a speed dating thing?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we prefer to call it 'rapid-engagement social networking for the romantically unattached.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why can't you just say singles?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because 'single' is a divisive term that suggests a lack of wholeness. We're all whole people, Alexis.

jokes.text: Did someone mention the posture thing? What? What is the utility shirts?
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if something is 'off' about how she looks
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately rattles off multiple things she's clearly been holding back — 'the posture thing' and 'the utility shirts' — as if there's a whole list
jokes.explanation: Wonderful Alexis moment. Her instant pivot to unsolicited fashion critique — 'the posture thing,' 'the utility shirts' — implies she's had these grievances queued up for a long time. The specificity and rapid-fire delivery are both very Alexis. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You stole my sweater!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I didn't steal it, I merely engaged in some light kleptomania. It's not a crime, it's a condition.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That literally is a crime.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I find your accusation rather pellucid and frankly, beneath us.

jokes.text: No, no. [beat] Hmm. And he suggested that I wear it every day. So, I'm having a really hard time not taking that personally.
jokes.setup: Alexis guesses Johnny gave Stevie something terrible; Stevie reveals it was a suitcase of makeup
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'No, no' reaction to the reveal, followed by the flat 'I'm having a really hard time not taking that personally,' is played with perfect deadpan — she is obviously taking it very personally
jokes.explanation: Stevie's controlled devastation over the makeup gift is a great character moment. The understated 'I'm having a really hard time not taking that personally' when she clearly has already taken it very personally is prime Stevie deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I carried the idea. I gestated it, I nurtured it, and I gave birth to it in front of a room full of people. That makes it my baby.

jokes.text: Here's the thing you should know about my Dad, sweetest little guy, gives the worst gifts. Like he built David a basketball court for his Bar Mitzvah.
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining Johnny's gift-giving history to contextualize the makeup briefcase
jokes.punchline: The punchline is the basketball court for a Bar Mitzvah — completely wrong gift for David, who clearly has no interest in basketball
jokes.explanation: Great Rose family backstory joke. The basketball court for David's Bar Mitzvah — a gift that is maximally wrong for David specifically — is excellent character specificity. This is the setup for an even better joke to follow.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is going to be just like Judy and Lorna!

jokes.text: David forced us to watch him shoot at a basket for 10 minutes, until the ball hit the rim, and then bounced back in his face. But it actually worked out perfectly, because all David really wanted for his Bar Mitzvah, was a nose job. They took the court down the next day.
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining how to train Johnny out of bad gifts — David made him watch the basketball fail
jokes.punchline: The ball bouncing back to hit David in the face — accidentally getting him the nose job he actually wanted — is a perfect escalating payoff. The court came down the next day.
jokes.explanation: The episode's strongest joke. The setup (basketball court for David), escalation (David making them watch him fail for 10 minutes), and payoff (ball hits him in face, gives him the nose job he wanted, court comes down next day) is a perfectly constructed three-act joke in two sentences. Every beat earns the next. It's deeply specific to the Rose family, completely plausible within their world, and the 'They took the court down the next day' button is pitch-perfect. This approaches the 9+ anchor quality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you know who Judy Garland and Lorna Luft are?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, no. Should I?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis!

jokes.text: Oh, and also, he gave me a diamond tennis bracelet for my Sweet 16. That's bad. I know.
jokes.setup: Alexis is listing Johnny's history of bad gifts
jokes.punchline: A diamond tennis bracelet sounds like a great gift — but in the context of this wealthy family, it's apparently a missed-mark gift; Stevie's 'That's bad' / Alexis's 'I know' confirms the joke inversion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion joke. The audience expects Stevie to say 'that's great' — but both she and Alexis agree a diamond tennis bracelet is a bad gift, which tells you everything about the Rose family's former wealth and expectations. The two-word exchange ('That's bad.' / 'I know.') is perfectly economical. Great character-world building disguised as a simple joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to go find an actual olive branch and just hand it to you silently.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's the most David thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: I just did. I'm happy to finish doing the creams, I... I don't think it's a two-person job. Okay, so you're just gonna stay here and not have lunch then, or are we going in shifts?
jokes.setup: David and Patrick are trying to navigate being 'just co-workers' which neither actually wants
jokes.punchline: The mundane logistics of the store (finishing the creams, going to lunch 'in shifts') played against the clearly unresolved romantic tension is both cringe-funny and touching
jokes.explanation: Competent cringe comedy. The idea of going to lunch 'in shifts' as co-workers when they're clearly more is a good absurdist detail. Not spectacular but serves the scene well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't believe I just did that in front of everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you did it in front of literally everyone, so...

jokes.text: I don't want to get used to this! I don't wanna pretend like we're co-workers. We are co-workers. Okay, I just liked it better when we were more than that.
jokes.setup: Patrick suggests they focus on business as co-workers
jokes.punchline: David's outburst — 'I don't wanna pretend like we're co-workers' — followed by Patrick's flat 'We are co-workers' is a sharp comedic beat that also carries genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: Solid comedic beat that also carries real feeling. Patrick's 'We are co-workers' as a flat correction to David's romantic framing is perfectly timed — the laugh and the ache land simultaneously. Good economy.

jokes.text: I've never been in this situation before. Where someone's been so nice... to me. And generous.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks why David didn't speak up sooner
jokes.punchline: David's vulnerable admission that being treated well is an entirely foreign experience to him — delivered with genuine pathos but also as a comedy beat about his privileged yet emotionally neglected background
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's more emotionally resonant comedy moments. The admission that no one has ever been 'so nice' to David is both funny (given his wealthy background) and genuinely touching. The pause before 'to me' is perfect. High character integration — this only works because of David's established arc.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, were you holding back on talking to me because you were getting gifts? I was very upset, and confused. So upset, that I barely finished the chocolates.
jokes.setup: Patrick realizes David was stringing out the reconciliation to receive more gifts
jokes.punchline: 'I was very upset... so upset that I barely finished the chocolates' — the qualifier 'barely' does all the work, confirming he ate them all while claiming devastation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two or three best jokes. 'So upset that I barely finished the chocolates' is a beautifully constructed confession — the word 'barely' is doing incredible comic work, implying he absolutely did finish them. The structure is impeccable: earnest claim of distress, then immediate self-undermining detail. Deeply David. This is in the territory of the 8.0 anchor — highly quotable, perfectly economical, character-specific.

jokes.text: David, this wasn't meant to be some Advent calendar of apologies, it was like an olive branch to get you to talk to me.
jokes.setup: David has been treating Patrick's stream of gifts as an ongoing reward for delayed forgiveness
jokes.punchline: 'Advent calendar of apologies' is a perfect metaphor — implying David was opening one gift per day as if it were December — and the show's title 'The Olive Branch' is directly referenced
jokes.explanation: 'Advent calendar of apologies' is exceptional writing — original, perfectly structured, and immediately vivid. It also serves as the episode's thematic anchor (olive branch). The metaphor is so precise and specific that it elevates what could be a standard romantic comedy scene. Earns its 8+ score — comparable to the quality of the reference anchors.

jokes.text: I just, I guess, didn't know, how many olive branches you were planning on extending. Ideally, one!
jokes.setup: Patrick explains the gifts were one olive branch
jokes.punchline: David's 'I didn't know how many you were planning' is a mock-reasonable defense; Patrick's 'Ideally, one!' is the exasperated correction
jokes.explanation: Strong callback exchange. David's sophistic defense ('I didn't know how many') and Patrick's exasperated 'Ideally, one!' is fast, funny, and deepens the olive branch motif. Good economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, and now I know, that while I was torturing myself, you were sitting at home, just opening gifts.
jokes.setup: David has confessed he was enjoying the gifts while Patrick suffered
jokes.punchline: Patrick's flat summation — 'you were sitting at home, just opening gifts' — is both an accusation and a comedy beat
jokes.explanation: Good comedic beat. Patrick's controlled outrage delivered as understatement is in character. The image of David 'just opening gifts' while Patrick suffered is funny. Slightly less powerful because the joke has already been made several times.

jokes.text: I see you like the bracelet. Love the bracelet, thank you.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just scolded David for enjoying the gifts
jokes.punchline: He clocks David touching/wearing the bracelet; David immediately, cheerfully confirms — 'Love the bracelet, thank you' — no shame whatsoever
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback button. David's instant 'Love the bracelet, thank you' — cheerful, shameless, in the middle of being scolded — is a perfect character moment. Maximum economy. The callback lands perfectly because we've been waiting for it since [01:38].

jokes.text: I was lying about the chocolates, I ate them all. But I'm sure there are some flowers that are still alive.
jokes.setup: David offers to return some of the gifts
jokes.punchline: He immediately confesses he ate all the chocolates (reversing his earlier 'barely finished' claim) and offers possibly-dead flowers instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. The confession 'I was lying about the chocolates, I ate them all' is the definitive version of the chocolate joke — funnier than the setup — and 'there are some flowers that are still alive' is a wonderfully grim secondary offer. Earns its 8 score.

jokes.text: You know what, I feel like now maybe I deserve an olive branch, or two? Okay, I understand that. What if... I gave you back some of the olive branches that you gave to me?
jokes.setup: Patrick says maybe he deserves an olive branch now
jokes.punchline: David proposes returning some of the gifts Patrick gave HIM as his olive branch — a delightfully cheap and circular offer
jokes.explanation: Great character joke. David's proposal to re-gift Patrick's own presents back to him as his olive branch is wonderfully shameless and very David. The olive branch metaphor continues to build. High character integration.

jokes.text: You know what I'm gonna do? I'm gonna go to lunch, and you're gonna sit here, and think about what you've done.
jokes.setup: The reconciliation has been reached, but Patrick wants to establish something
jokes.punchline: Patrick's mock-parental 'sit here and think about what you've done' — treating a romantic reconciliation like a timeout — is funny and sweet simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Warm, funny button on the reconciliation. The parental framing is a playful power dynamic between them that feels fresh and character-specific.

jokes.text: Um, while you're at lunch, can you get me some lunch? Unbelievable.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just left David to 'think about what he's done'
jokes.punchline: David immediately asks Patrick to bring him lunch — completely undercutting the contrition of the moment — and Patrick's 'Unbelievable' is the perfect button
jokes.explanation: Excellent scene button. David asking for lunch in the same breath as his timeout punishment is absolutely perfect character comedy — he has zero self-awareness about how this looks, and Patrick's 'Unbelievable' captures exactly what the audience feels (in a delighted way). Very high character integration. One of the episode's best joke structures.

jokes.text: Wow! Look at you! Yeah, used some of that makeup you gave me. Oh, is that what it is? Ah, I thought it was the shoes.
jokes.setup: Stevie appears wearing the makeup from Johnny's gift, clearly having overdone it
jokes.punchline: Johnny sees her and says he thought it was 'the shoes' that transformed her look — completely ignoring the obvious makeup, which implies the effect is either unrecognizable or alarming
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy beat. Johnny's oblivious 'I thought it was the shoes' when Stevie is clearly wearing conspicuous makeup is a lovely bit of Johnny cluelessness that also deepens the gift-giving storyline.

jokes.text: You know, something about this feels really right. Hmm. I can't believe that I didn't think to buy all this for myself!
jokes.setup: Stevie has tried on the makeup and is reconsidering her original outrage
jokes.punchline: She admits she loves it and is annoyed she didn't think of it herself — completely reversing her earlier mortification
jokes.explanation: Good character reversal. Stevie's 180 on the makeup gift is funny because it validates Johnny's instinct while completely undermining her own pride. The warm resolution of the storyline.

jokes.text: So, I'm happy to man the desk for you this afternoon, if you've got stuff to do. Oh, no, no, I... I can keep working here. No, Mr. Rose, I insist. I feel like I'm long overdue for some quality, like, face time with the guests.
jokes.setup: Stevie is now wearing conspicuous makeup and wants to be seen
jokes.punchline: She insists on 'face time with the guests' — a business term that also literally means showing off her new face — and the inversion of her original reluctance is the joke
jokes.explanation: Decent double meaning with 'face time' doing light wordplay duty. The reversal of Stevie's attitude is complete and funny, though not exceptionally crafted.

jokes.text: You know what the great thing about this is? Right after work, I can go do a quick shift over at Bazonga's Gentleman's Lounge.
jokes.setup: Johnny is complimenting Stevie's look
jokes.punchline: Stevie announces she can now work at 'Bazonga's Gentleman's Lounge' after her shift — implying the makeup makes her look like she belongs at a strip club or similar establishment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Bazonga's Gentleman's Lounge' is a perfect invented name — specific, absurd, and immediately evocative. Stevie's dry delivery of this as a legitimate career option is very in-character. The name alone is quotable. High character integration and originality.

jokes.text: All right, I'm getting the hint. Hint? What hint?
jokes.setup: Stevie has been subtly signaling the makeup is too much for her
jokes.punchline: Johnny says he's 'getting the hint' — Stevie's straight-faced 'What hint?' implies she was being completely sincere about Bazonga's
jokes.explanation: Good button on the Bazonga's joke. Stevie's 'What hint?' suggests she may have been completely sincere, which is funnier than if it were a transparent hint.

jokes.text: Well, if you don't mind, I'm gonna step outside. I'm getting flashbacks of David attempting a 3-pointer.
jokes.setup: Johnny exits after the Stevie makeup situation resolves
jokes.punchline: He says Stevie's overdone makeup is giving him flashbacks to David's failed basketball — a callback to the Bar Mitzvah story, implying both were equally traumatic failed gift experiments
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that connects the two gift-giving storylines (Stevie's makeup, David's court). Johnny's recognition that both were disasters is self-aware and funny. The flashback framing implies genuine trauma.

jokes.text: I wouldn't let them have it. Not until they accepted my demand, which was to employ the services of the CEO and Founder of Alexis Rose - whatever you called it - as part of my advisory team.
jokes.setup: Moira tells Alexis that Council chose her idea
jokes.punchline: 'whatever you called it' — Moira can't remember the name of Alexis's company despite just championing it — undercuts the heroic framing of her gesture
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira. She went to bat for Alexis's company and doesn't know its name. The specific parenthetical 'whatever you called it' is perfectly placed — the throwaway nature of it is the joke.

jokes.text: Hmm, well we landed on a one-of-a-kind event to celebrate and... congregate those who happen to be unattached, uh, in the hopes of facilitating interpersonal connections. Sorry, so like, a singles event, then?
jokes.setup: Moira describes the Council-approved event in maximum grandiose language
jokes.punchline: Alexis cuts through all the verbiage with 'so like, a singles event, then?' — the contrast between Moira's elaborate circumlocution and Alexis's blunt accuracy is the joke
jokes.explanation: Very good dynamic. Moira's elaborate euphemism for 'singles event' is funny on its own, and Alexis's deflating translation is the perfect comedic response. Good character contrast.

jokes.text: That word is now considered derogatory. I believe they prefer to be called independents.
jokes.setup: Alexis calls it a 'singles event'
jokes.punchline: Moira declares 'singles' derogatory and insists on 'independents' — a mock-progressive language stance that is both pompous and absurd
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira joke. Her declaration that 'singles' is derogatory and 'independents' is preferred mimics the comedy of progressive language policing applied absurdly. 'I believe they prefer to be called independents' is delivered with total authority. Quotable and very Moira.

jokes.text: So you stole my idea, took it to Council, and claimed it as your own?
jokes.setup: Moira has taken Alexis's singles event idea to Council without telling her
jokes.punchline: Alexis's flat accusation cuts through Moira's elaborate spin — the directness vs. Moira's evasion is the comedic contrast
jokes.explanation: Competent but not especially crafted. The accusation is funny in context but fairly predictable — it's setting up Moira's response, which carries more of the comedic weight.

jokes.text: Alexis, now is not the time for pettifogging!
jokes.setup: Alexis accuses Moira of stealing her idea
jokes.punchline: Moira responds with 'pettifogging' — an archaic legal term meaning quibbling over minor points — deployed as though Alexis is being unreasonable about idea theft
jokes.explanation: 'Pettifogging!' as a response to being caught stealing someone's idea is spectacular Moira. The word is real but so archaic and specific that it feels invented. Deployed here to dismiss legitimate grievance as nitpicking — perfectly in character. One of the episode's most quotable single lines. Earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: Singles Week just happened to be the idea that received a lot of traction. Singles Week? I was pitching a Singles Night.
jokes.setup: Moira defends herself saying she just pushed what got traction
jokes.punchline: Alexis points out Moira also expanded her one-night idea into a full week — she didn't just steal it, she supersized it
jokes.explanation: Good comedic beat. The distinction between 'Night' and 'Week' lands because Moira didn't just steal — she also inflated, making the theft worse. Quick, punchy exchange.

jokes.text: No, that was merely an example of how Rosewood would similarly put us on the map. ... Well then, I took your little germ of an idea, and I fertilized it to fruition.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to explain/justify how she used Alexis's idea
jokes.punchline: 'I took your little germ of an idea, and I fertilized it to fruition' — the language of reproduction applied to intellectual property theft is both absurd and very Moira
jokes.explanation: 'Fertilized it to fruition' is a great Moira construction — unintentionally suggesting she impregnated Alexis's idea. The biological metaphor for idea theft is both grandiose and absurd, and the confidence with which it's delivered makes it funnier. Quotable.

jokes.text: No, that was merely an example... No, no, no, you specifically referenced a small shire in Scotland... Ireland! ... that had a very lucrative week-long singles event. No, that is where I got the idea for singles night. I was proposing like, a matchmaking event at the café.
jokes.setup: Moira claims she heard Alexis propose a 'week-long singles event'
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects both the country (Ireland, not Scotland — consistent with Moira's earlier Council gaffe) and reveals her original pitch was just a café matchmaking night
jokes.explanation: Good consistency callback. Moira still says Scotland; Alexis corrects to Ireland again. The repeat of the error confirms it's a genuine Moira blind spot, not a one-time slip. Serves character continuity.

jokes.text: Starting to get keyed up about this prospect of a mother-daughter power team. Modern day Judy and Lorna.
jokes.setup: Moira proposes she and Alexis work together as Co-Chairs
jokes.punchline: 'Modern day Judy and Lorna' — Judy Garland and Lorna Luft (mother and daughter) — a reference so specific and theatrical that Alexis has no idea who she's talking about
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira character comedy. The 'Judy and Lorna' reference (Judy Garland and daughter Lorna Luft) is so Moira — theatrical, showbiz-specific, and completely inaccessible to Alexis. 'I don't know who that is' / 'Oh my god' is a perfect two-beat button. High character integration.

jokes.text: I don't know who that is. Oh my god.
jokes.setup: Moira references Judy Garland and Lorna Luft as their template
jokes.punchline: Alexis has no idea who they are; Moira's 'Oh my god' is equal parts horror and resignation
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the Judy/Lorna joke. The exchange is three words each and communicates volumes about the Moira/Alexis generational/cultural divide. Economy is very high.

jokes.text: Modern day Judy and Lorna. / I don't know who that is. / Oh my god.
jokes.setup: Moira references Judy Garland and Lorna Luft as their mother-daughter role models
jokes.punchline: Alexis has no idea who they are; Moira's 'Oh my god' captures generational horror
jokes.explanation: Alexis's ignorance and Moira's horror is a clean two-beat joke. Moira's 'Oh my god' is its own comedy — the depth of her despair at not being understood is perfectly in character. Strong economy.

jokes.text: What is this? Consider this my olive branch. [Patrick has arranged a romantic scene with 'Simply the Best' playing]
jokes.setup: David arrives and finds Patrick has set up a surprise
jokes.punchline: Patrick calls it 'my olive branch' — completing the episode's central motif — and the song 'Simply the Best' plays, which has been a running romantic callback throughout the series
jokes.explanation: The emotional/comedic payoff of the episode. 'Simply the Best' is the series' romantic touchstone for David and Patrick, making this both a joke and a genuine heartfelt moment. The 'olive branch' callback closes the episode's thematic arc. Rewatch bonus because the series significance amplifies on repeat viewing.

jokes.text: You know people can see you, right?
jokes.setup: Patrick is singing/performing along to 'Simply the Best' in a romantic gesture
jokes.punchline: David, touched but still himself, interrupts the romantic moment with a gently dry observation that Patrick is being publicly visible
jokes.explanation: Beautiful line to close the sequence. In the middle of a fully sincere romantic moment, David can't help being David — slightly mortified, dry, and unable to just receive the gesture without a comment. 'You know people can see you, right?' is perfect economy and perfectly in character. It's also the funniest possible response to a heartfelt serenade. Earns its 8 score — comparable to the anchor.

tmdb_overview: David tries to reconcile with Patrick. Moira pitches a singles event at Town Hall while Johnny buys Stevie a gift for her help at the motel.
insight: Family reconciliation fuels 45 jokes across tense negotiation scenes.
